quizQuestion: Which dinosaur is famous for its enormous jaws, tiny arms, and banana-sized teeth?
explanation: Tyrannosaurus rex was the apex predator of the Late Cretaceous, living 68-66 million years ago in western North America. Its teeth reached about 30cm and had a bite force exceeding 12,000 pounds. Source: Wikipedia/Tyrannosaurus
statement: Tyrannosaurus rex is famous for its enormous jaws, tiny arms, and banana-sized teeth.
wowFactor: T. rex had the strongest bite force of any land animal ever recorded — powerful enough to crush solid bone.
visualDescription: Massive bipedal predator with enormous toothed skull, tiny vestigial forelimbs, and powerful legs, standing in a Late Cretaceous landscape

quizQuestion: In which geological period did Tyrannosaurus rex live?
explanation: Tyrannosaurus rex lived during the Late Cretaceous period, approximately 68-66 million years ago, going extinct at the Cretaceous-Paleogene (K-Pg) mass extinction event. Source: Wikipedia/Tyrannosaurus
statement: Tyrannosaurus rex lived during the Late Cretaceous period, 68-66 million years ago.
wowFactor: T. rex lived closer in time to us than to Stegosaurus — separated from Stegosaurus by 80 million years.
visualDescription: T. rex silhouette against a Cretaceous sky with ferns and flowering plants below

quizQuestion: Approximately how many meters long was Tyrannosaurus rex?
explanation: Tyrannosaurus rex reached approximately 12 meters in length, making it one of the largest land predators ever. It weighed around 8.4 tonnes. Source: Wikipedia/Tyrannosaurus
statement: Tyrannosaurus rex was approximately 12 meters long and weighed about 8.4 tonnes.
wowFactor: At 12 meters long and 8.4 tonnes, T. rex was roughly the size and weight of a large school bus.
visualDescription: T. rex silhouette with a scale bar showing 12 meters in length beside a human figure for comparison

quizQuestion: What is the name of the famous T. rex specimen on display at the Field Museum in Chicago?
explanation: The T. rex specimen nicknamed 'Sue' (FMNH PR 2081) is one of the largest and most complete T. rex skeletons ever found. It is displayed at the Field Museum of Natural History in Chicago. Source: Wikipedia/Tyrannosaurus
statement: The famous T. rex specimen 'Sue' is displayed at the Field Museum in Chicago.
wowFactor: Sue was purchased at auction in 1997 for $8.36 million — a record price for a dinosaur fossil at the time.
visualDescription: Enormous T. rex skeleton mounted in a grand museum hall, its massive skull at eye level with visitors below

quizQuestion: Which dinosaur was the largest carnivorous dinosaur known, featuring a massive neural spine sail on its back?
explanation: Spinosaurus was the largest known carnivorous dinosaur, reaching about 14 meters. It lived 99-93 million years ago in North Africa and had a distinctive sail formed by neural spines up to 1.65 meters tall. Source: Wikipedia/Spinosaurus
statement: Spinosaurus was the largest carnivorous dinosaur known, with a massive sail up to 1.65 meters tall on its back.
wowFactor: Spinosaurus was semi-aquatic — a 2020 study revealed it had a paddle-like tail for swimming, making it the only known dinosaur adapted for aquatic life.
visualDescription: Enormous crocodile-snouted dinosaur wading through a river delta, its massive dorsal sail rising above the water, catching North African sunlight

quizQuestion: In which geological period did Spinosaurus live?
explanation: Spinosaurus lived during the Late Cretaceous period, approximately 99-93 million years ago, in what is now Morocco and Egypt in North Africa. Source: Wikipedia/Spinosaurus
statement: Spinosaurus lived during the Late Cretaceous period, 99-93 million years ago, in North Africa.
wowFactor: Spinosaurus and Carcharodontosaurus lived at the same time in the same region of North Africa — two giant predators sharing the same ecosystem.
visualDescription: Spinosaurus in a lush Cretaceous North African river delta environment with mangrove-like trees

quizQuestion: Approximately how many meters long was Spinosaurus?
explanation: Spinosaurus reached approximately 14 meters in length, making it longer than Tyrannosaurus rex. It weighed around 7.4 tonnes. Source: Wikipedia/Spinosaurus
statement: Spinosaurus was approximately 14 meters long — longer than Tyrannosaurus rex.
wowFactor: Spinosaurus was longer than T. rex but was a fish-eater, not primarily a land predator — it spent much of its time in rivers.
visualDescription: Spinosaurus length compared to T. rex with scale markers, showing Spinosaurus extends noticeably further

quizQuestion: What was the primary diet of Spinosaurus, unlike most large theropods?
explanation: Spinosaurus was primarily a fish-eater (piscivore), evidenced by its narrow crocodile-like snout, conical teeth, and semi-aquatic adaptations. It is unlike most large theropods that hunted land animals. Source: Wikipedia/Spinosaurus
statement: Spinosaurus primarily ate fish — unlike most large theropods which hunted land animals.
wowFactor: The largest carnivorous dinosaur ever was a fisherman — Spinosaurus waded into rivers and caught fish with its crocodile-like jaws.
visualDescription: Spinosaurus lunging into a river to catch a large fish, its narrow jaws snatching prey from the water

quizQuestion: Which Late Jurassic apex predator is the most common large carnivore found in the Morrison Formation of North America?
explanation: Allosaurus was the apex predator of the Late Jurassic period, 155-150 million years ago, and is extremely abundant in the Morrison Formation of the western United States. It was named 'different lizard' by Othniel Charles Marsh. Source: Wikipedia/Allosaurus
statement: Allosaurus was the apex predator of the Late Jurassic, abundant in the Morrison Formation of North America.
wowFactor: Allosaurus may have hunted giant sauropods by ambushing them and using its skull like a hatchet — slashing rather than biting.
visualDescription: Powerful three-clawed predator charging through a Late Jurassic landscape of conifers and ferns, massive skull low to the ground

quizQuestion: In which geological period did Allosaurus live?
explanation: Allosaurus lived during the Late Jurassic period, approximately 155-150 million years ago. It coexisted with sauropods like Diplodocus and Brachiosaurus in the Morrison Formation. Source: Wikipedia/Allosaurus
statement: Allosaurus lived during the Late Jurassic period, 155-150 million years ago.
wowFactor: Allosaurus lived 80 million years before T. rex — and would have been just as unfamiliar with T. rex as we are.
visualDescription: Allosaurus in a Jurassic fern-filled forest landscape with giant cycads and conifers

quizQuestion: Approximately how many meters long was Allosaurus?
explanation: Allosaurus reached approximately 8.5 meters in length and weighed around 2.3 tonnes. While smaller than T. rex, it was the dominant predator of its Jurassic ecosystem. Source: Wikipedia/Allosaurus
statement: Allosaurus was approximately 8.5 meters long and weighed around 2.3 tonnes.
wowFactor: Despite being significantly smaller than T. rex, Allosaurus had three large claws on each arm — weapons T. rex could only dream of.
visualDescription: Allosaurus skeleton mount showing its 8.5-meter length with three-clawed forelimbs prominent

quizQuestion: Which small feathered dinosaur from Mongolia had a sickle-shaped killing claw on each foot?
explanation: Velociraptor was a small feathered dinosaur from Mongolia, about 1.8 meters long and weighing only 15 kg. It had a distinctive curved sickle claw on its second toe. Source: Wikipedia/Velociraptor
statement: Velociraptor was a small feathered dinosaur from Mongolia with a sickle-shaped killing claw on each foot.
wowFactor: The raptors in Jurassic Park were actually based on Deinonychus — real Velociraptors were only turkey-sized and fully feathered.
visualDescription: Turkey-sized feathered dinosaur with a large curved sickle claw raised on its second toe, shown in the Mongolian Gobi Desert

quizQuestion: In which geological period did Velociraptor live?
explanation: Velociraptor lived during the Late Cretaceous period, approximately 75-71 million years ago, in what is now Mongolia and China. Source: Wikipedia/Velociraptor
statement: Velociraptor lived during the Late Cretaceous period, 75-71 million years ago, in Mongolia and China.
wowFactor: Velociraptor lived at the same time as T. rex — they were contemporaries in the Late Cretaceous, just in different continents.
visualDescription: Velociraptor in a dry Cretaceous Mongolian Gobi Desert landscape

quizQuestion: Which dinosaur was actually turkey-sized in real life, despite being portrayed as human-sized in Jurassic Park?
explanation: Real Velociraptors were about 1.8 meters long and weighed only 15 kg — roughly turkey-sized. The Jurassic Park 'raptors' were actually based on Deinonychus, a much larger relative at 3.4 meters. Source: Wikipedia/Velociraptor
statement: Real Velociraptors were turkey-sized — the Jurassic Park raptors were based on Deinonychus.
wowFactor: Michael Crichton used 'Velociraptor' in his novel because it sounded better — he actually meant Deinonychus, which was nearly twice the size.
visualDescription: Side-by-side size comparison of a real turkey-sized Velociraptor versus the human-sized Jurassic Park depiction

quizQuestion: Which Late Jurassic theropod had a distinctive horn on its nose, giving it the name 'horned lizard'?
explanation: Ceratosaurus was a Late Jurassic predator with a distinctive nasal horn, giving it its name meaning 'horned lizard'. It also had a row of osteoderms along its back and coexisted with Allosaurus in the Morrison Formation. Source: Wikipedia/Ceratosaurus
statement: Ceratosaurus had a distinctive horn on its nose, giving it the name 'horned lizard'. It coexisted with Allosaurus in the Morrison Formation.
wowFactor: Ceratosaurus was the only theropod with a nasal horn and a row of bony armor plates along its back — making it unique among carnivorous dinosaurs.
visualDescription: Medium-sized theropod with a prominent nasal horn and a row of osteoderms along its spine, stalking through Jurassic forest undergrowth

quizQuestion: In which geological period did Ceratosaurus live?
explanation: Ceratosaurus lived during the Late Jurassic period, approximately 153-148 million years ago. It coexisted with Allosaurus and the giant sauropods of the Morrison Formation. Source: Wikipedia/Ceratosaurus
statement: Ceratosaurus lived during the Late Jurassic period, 153-148 million years ago, alongside Allosaurus.
wowFactor: Two large theropods — Allosaurus and Ceratosaurus — competed for prey in the same ecosystem 150 million years ago.
visualDescription: Ceratosaurus and Allosaurus both present in a Late Jurassic Morrison Formation landscape

quizQuestion: Approximately how many meters long was Ceratosaurus?
explanation: Ceratosaurus was approximately 6 meters long and weighed around 0.5 tonnes. This made it smaller than its contemporary Allosaurus. Source: Wikipedia/Ceratosaurus
statement: Ceratosaurus was approximately 6 meters long — smaller than its contemporary Allosaurus at 8.5 meters.
wowFactor: Despite being smaller than Allosaurus, Ceratosaurus had four-fingered hands — unusual for theropods, most of which had three or fewer fingers.
visualDescription: Ceratosaurus skeleton showing four-fingered hands and nasal horn, labeled with 6-meter scale bar

quizQuestion: Which Argentinian theropod had bull-like horns above its eyes and even tinier arms than T. rex?
explanation: Carnotaurus was a Late Cretaceous predator from Argentina with distinctive bull-like horns above its eyes. It had the most vestigial forelimbs of any large theropod — even more reduced than T. rex's arms. Source: Wikipedia/Carnotaurus
statement: Carnotaurus had bull-like horns above its eyes and the most vestigial forelimbs of any large theropod.
wowFactor: Carnotaurus may have been one of the fastest large predators ever — estimated at 48-56 km/h, and its skin impressions show it had a bumpy, pebbly texture.
visualDescription: Muscular predator with two prominent bull-like horns above its eyes charging forward, its tiny stub-like forelimbs useless against its powerful body

quizQuestion: In which geological period did Carnotaurus live?
explanation: Carnotaurus lived during the Late Cretaceous period, approximately 72-69 million years ago, in what is now Argentina. It was a member of the Abelisauridae family. Source: Wikipedia/Carnotaurus
statement: Carnotaurus lived during the Late Cretaceous period, 72-69 million years ago, in Argentina.
wowFactor: Carnotaurus is one of the best-known theropods from South America — and the first carnivorous dinosaur known from fossil skin impressions.
visualDescription: Carnotaurus silhouette against a Late Cretaceous Patagonian landscape of sparse conifers

quizQuestion: Approximately how many meters long was Carnotaurus?
explanation: Carnotaurus was approximately 8 meters long and weighed around 1.3 tonnes. Source: Wikipedia/Carnotaurus
statement: Carnotaurus was approximately 8 meters long and weighed around 1.3 tonnes.
wowFactor: Carnotaurus weighed only 1.3 tonnes but may have run at nearly 50 km/h — a speed unmatched by most predators of its size.
visualDescription: Carnotaurus running at full sprint, body low and horizontal, horned head forward, tiny arms folded against its sides

quizQuestion: Which Early Jurassic theropod had a distinctive double crest on its skull, giving it the name 'two-crested lizard'?
explanation: Dilophosaurus had two parallel crests running along its skull, giving it the name 'two-crested lizard'. It was one of the earliest large predatory dinosaurs, living about 193 million years ago in what is now Arizona. Source: Wikipedia/Dilophosaurus
statement: Dilophosaurus had a distinctive double crest on its skull, making it one of the earliest large predatory dinosaurs.
wowFactor: Dilophosaurus had NO frill and NO venom — those were fictional inventions by Michael Crichton for Jurassic Park, with no basis in the fossil record.
visualDescription: Graceful Early Jurassic predator with two parallel crests along its skull, no frill or venom sac in sight, stalking through an Arizona desert landscape

quizQuestion: In which geological period did Dilophosaurus live?
explanation: Dilophosaurus lived during the Early Jurassic period, approximately 193 million years ago. It was one of the earliest large predatory dinosaurs, found in Arizona and China. Source: Wikipedia/Dilophosaurus
statement: Dilophosaurus lived during the Early Jurassic period, approximately 193 million years ago.
wowFactor: Dilophosaurus is one of the oldest large theropods known — it lived 45 million years before Allosaurus and 125 million years before T. rex.
visualDescription: Dilophosaurus in an Early Jurassic Arizona desert with sparse vegetation and red rock formations

quizQuestion: Which dinosaur was incorrectly portrayed as having a neck frill and venomous spit in Jurassic Park, features invented by the author?
explanation: Dilophosaurus was portrayed in Jurassic Park with a foldable neck frill and venom-spitting ability. These features have no basis in the fossil record and were invented by Michael Crichton. The real Dilophosaurus was simply a crested predator. Source: Wikipedia/Dilophosaurus
statement: Dilophosaurus had no frill or venom — those features were invented by Michael Crichton for Jurassic Park.
wowFactor: The Jurassic Park Dilophosaurus is depicted as small enough to fit in a Jeep — the real animal was 7 meters long and 400 kg.
visualDescription: Real Dilophosaurus fossil skull with twin crests but no frill socket, contrasted with a fictional Jurassic Park-style depiction struck through with an X

quizQuestion: Which chicken-sized dinosaur found in Germany is one of the smallest known non-avian dinosaurs?
explanation: Compsognathus was approximately 65 cm long and weighed about 3.5 kg — roughly chicken-sized. It was found in the Solnhofen limestone of Germany, the same deposits that preserved Archaeopteryx. Source: Wikipedia/Compsognathus
statement: Compsognathus was approximately chicken-sized (65 cm), one of the smallest known non-avian dinosaurs, found in Germany.
wowFactor: Compsognathus was found in the same Bavarian limestone that preserved Archaeopteryx — giving scientists a direct look at both the ancestor of birds and its tiny contemporary predator.
visualDescription: Tiny agile dinosaur roughly the size of a chicken, darting through a Late Jurassic European island environment with shallow lagoons

quizQuestion: In which geological period did Compsognathus live?
explanation: Compsognathus lived during the Late Jurassic period, approximately 150 million years ago, in what are now Germany and France. Source: Wikipedia/Compsognathus
statement: Compsognathus lived during the Late Jurassic period, approximately 150 million years ago, in Germany and France.
wowFactor: Compsognathus and Archaeopteryx — the earliest known bird — lived at the same time in the same place, giving scientists a rare snapshot of early bird evolution.
visualDescription: Compsognathus on a Late Jurassic European limestone island shore with an Archaeopteryx perched nearby in a tree

quizQuestion: Approximately how many meters long was Compsognathus?
explanation: Compsognathus was approximately 0.65 meters long (65 cm) — roughly the size of a chicken. Source: Wikipedia/Compsognathus
statement: Compsognathus was approximately 0.65 meters long — roughly the size of a chicken.
wowFactor: Compsognathus is so small that 18 of them would fit end-to-end to equal the length of a Tyrannosaurus rex.
visualDescription: Tiny Compsognathus with a scale bar showing 65 cm, a chicken shown beside it at the same scale for comparison

quizQuestion: Which Early Cretaceous dromaeosaurid, named 'terrible claw', was the actual inspiration for the Jurassic Park Velociraptors?
explanation: Deinonychus was the real-world basis for the Jurassic Park 'Velociraptors'. At 3.4 meters long and 73 kg, it was far larger than Velociraptor. John Ostrom's 1969 description of Deinonychus revolutionized paleontology by suggesting dinosaurs were active and warm-blooded. Source: Wikipedia/Deinonychus
statement: Deinonychus was the actual basis for Jurassic Park's 'Velociraptors' — it was much larger than the real Velociraptor.
wowFactor: John Ostrom's 1969 description of Deinonychus sparked the 'Dinosaur Renaissance' — completely changing how scientists viewed dinosaurs as active, intelligent animals.
visualDescription: Large feathered dromaeosaurid with an enormous sickle claw raised on its second toe, hunting in Early Cretaceous North American woodland

quizQuestion: In which geological period did Deinonychus live?
explanation: Deinonychus lived during the Early Cretaceous period, approximately 115-108 million years ago, in what is now Montana, Wyoming, and Oklahoma in the United States. Source: Wikipedia/Deinonychus
statement: Deinonychus lived during the Early Cretaceous period, 115-108 million years ago, in the western United States.
wowFactor: Deinonychus hunted prey much larger than itself — evidence of multiple Deinonychus alongside a large Tenontosaurus carcass suggests coordinated pack hunting.
visualDescription: Deinonychus in an Early Cretaceous forest clearing in what is now Montana

quizQuestion: Approximately how many meters long was Deinonychus?
explanation: Deinonychus was approximately 3.4 meters long and weighed about 73 kg. This is significantly larger than Velociraptor (1.8 m, 15 kg), which is why it was used as the basis for the Jurassic Park raptors. Source: Wikipedia/Deinonychus
statement: Deinonychus was approximately 3.4 meters long — nearly twice the length of Velociraptor.
wowFactor: At 3.4 meters and 73 kg, Deinonychus was nearly twice the size of a Velociraptor — much closer to the Jurassic Park portrayal.
visualDescription: Deinonychus at 3.4 meters shown beside a 1.8-meter Velociraptor for size comparison, both feathered

quizQuestion: Which South American theropod rivalled T. rex in size and likely hunted giant sauropods like Argentinosaurus?
explanation: Giganotosaurus was a massive predator from Patagonia, Argentina, approximately 12.5 meters long. Named in 1995, it rivals T. rex in size and likely preyed on giant sauropods like Argentinosaurus. Source: Wikipedia/Giganotosaurus
statement: Giganotosaurus from Patagonia rivalled T. rex in size and likely hunted giant sauropods like Argentinosaurus.
wowFactor: Giganotosaurus lived 30 million years before T. rex — and at 12.5 meters may have been slightly longer, though T. rex was more massive.
visualDescription: Massive elongated-skulled predator stalking through Patagonian Late Cretaceous plains, a giant Argentinosaurus visible in the background

quizQuestion: In which geological period did Giganotosaurus live?
explanation: Giganotosaurus lived during the Late Cretaceous period, approximately 98-97 million years ago, in what is now Patagonia, Argentina. Source: Wikipedia/Giganotosaurus
statement: Giganotosaurus lived during the Late Cretaceous period, approximately 98-97 million years ago, in Patagonia, Argentina.
wowFactor: Giganotosaurus lived about 30 million years before T. rex — while one dominated South America, the other had not yet evolved.
visualDescription: Giganotosaurus silhouette against a Cretaceous Patagonian landscape of open plains

quizQuestion: Approximately how many meters long was Giganotosaurus?
explanation: Giganotosaurus was approximately 12.5 meters long and weighed around 6.5 tonnes. Its elongated skull with blade-like serrated teeth distinguished it from T. rex. Source: Wikipedia/Giganotosaurus
statement: Giganotosaurus was approximately 12.5 meters long — potentially slightly longer than Tyrannosaurus rex.
wowFactor: Giganotosaurus had a slender, elongated skull unlike T. rex's deep box-like skull — a different predatory design for hunting different prey.
visualDescription: Giganotosaurus skull compared to T. rex skull, showing Giganotosaurus's slender elongated snout versus T. rex's deep robust skull

quizQuestion: Which North African theropod's name means 'shark-toothed lizard', after the serrated blade-like teeth similar to a great white shark?
explanation: Carcharodontosaurus gets its name from 'carcharias' (shark) and 'odontos' (tooth), referring to its serrated, blade-like teeth. It lived in North Africa 99-94 million years ago alongside Spinosaurus. Source: Wikipedia/Carcharodontosaurus
statement: Carcharodontosaurus means 'shark-toothed lizard' — named for its serrated blade-like teeth. It lived alongside Spinosaurus in North Africa.
wowFactor: Carcharodontosaurus and Spinosaurus both lived in the same region of North Africa at roughly the same time — creating one of the deadliest predator ecosystems in Earth's history.
visualDescription: Massive theropod with serrated blade-like teeth in an open-jawed threat display, North African Cretaceous landscape behind it

quizQuestion: In which geological period did Carcharodontosaurus live?
explanation: Carcharodontosaurus lived during the Late Cretaceous period, approximately 99-94 million years ago, in what are now Morocco and Algeria in North Africa. Source: Wikipedia/Carcharodontosaurus
statement: Carcharodontosaurus lived during the Late Cretaceous period, 99-94 million years ago, in Morocco and Algeria.
wowFactor: Carcharodontosaurus was first described in 1931 but the original fossils were destroyed in World War II — scientists had to rely on drawings until new specimens were found in the 1990s.
visualDescription: Carcharodontosaurus in a Cretaceous North African landscape

quizQuestion: Approximately how many meters long was Carcharodontosaurus?
explanation: Carcharodontosaurus reached approximately 12.5 meters in length and weighed around 6.2 tonnes, making it one of the largest carnivorous dinosaurs ever known. Source: Wikipedia/Carcharodontosaurus
statement: Carcharodontosaurus was approximately 12.5 meters long and weighed around 6.2 tonnes.
wowFactor: Carcharodontosaurus, Spinosaurus, and Giganotosaurus are all roughly the same enormous size — showing that multiple lineages independently evolved to T. rex scale.
visualDescription: Three massive theropod silhouettes in a row — Spinosaurus, Carcharodontosaurus, and T. rex — with scale bars showing their near-identical lengths

quizQuestion: Which tyrannosaurid dinosaur is named after the Canadian province where a bonebed of 26 individuals was discovered?
explanation: Albertosaurus is named after Alberta, Canada, where Barnum Brown discovered a bonebed containing 26 individuals in 1910. This is considered evidence of pack behavior. It was a smaller relative of T. rex. Source: Wikipedia/Albertosaurus
statement: Albertosaurus is named after Alberta, Canada. A bonebed of 26 individuals suggests it may have been a pack hunter.
wowFactor: A single site in Alberta contained 26 Albertosaurus individuals — the largest known accumulation of any tyrannosaur species in one place.
visualDescription: Lean tyrannosaur with a narrower skull than T. rex, moving through a Late Cretaceous Alberta forest of conifers and flowering plants

quizQuestion: In which geological period did Albertosaurus live?
explanation: Albertosaurus lived during the Late Cretaceous period, approximately 70-68 million years ago, in what is now Alberta, Canada. Source: Wikipedia/Albertosaurus
statement: Albertosaurus lived during the Late Cretaceous period, 70-68 million years ago, in Alberta, Canada.
wowFactor: Albertosaurus and T. rex were near-contemporaries — Albertosaurus lived just 2-4 million years before T. rex appeared.
visualDescription: Albertosaurus in a Late Cretaceous Alberta conifer forest

quizQuestion: Approximately how many meters long was Albertosaurus?
explanation: Albertosaurus was approximately 9 meters long and weighed around 1.3 tonnes — significantly smaller than its relative T. rex at 12 meters and 8.4 tonnes. Source: Wikipedia/Albertosaurus
statement: Albertosaurus was approximately 9 meters long — smaller than its relative T. rex at 12 meters.
wowFactor: Albertosaurus weighed only 1.3 tonnes versus T. rex's 8.4 tonnes — despite being over three-quarters as long, it was only a fraction of the weight.
visualDescription: Albertosaurus skeleton showing its slimmer, longer proportions compared to T. rex outline

quizQuestion: Which fish-eating spinosaurid discovered in Surrey, England has a name meaning 'heavy claw' from its enormous curved thumb claw?
explanation: Baryonyx means 'heavy claw', named for its large curved thumb claw. It was discovered in Surrey, England in 1983 and had a crocodile-like skull adapted for eating fish. Fish scales were found in its stomach contents. Source: Wikipedia/Baryonyx
statement: Baryonyx ('heavy claw') was a fish-eating spinosaurid discovered in Surrey, England in 1983, with fish scales in its stomach.
wowFactor: Baryonyx was found by an amateur fossil hunter named William Walker in a Surrey clay pit in 1983 — the claw alone was 31 cm long.
visualDescription: Spinosaurid with a long narrow crocodile-like snout holding a large fish, its enormous curved thumb claw raised, in an Early Cretaceous English wetland

quizQuestion: In which geological period did Baryonyx live?
explanation: Baryonyx lived during the Early Cretaceous period, approximately 130-125 million years ago, in England, Spain, and Portugal. Source: Wikipedia/Baryonyx
statement: Baryonyx lived during the Early Cretaceous period, 130-125 million years ago, in England, Spain, and Portugal.
wowFactor: Baryonyx shows that giant fish-eating spinosaurids weren't limited to Africa — they spread across Europe during the Early Cretaceous when Britain was a warm coastal wetland.
visualDescription: Baryonyx wading in an Early Cretaceous English wetland, surrounded by ferns and early flowering plants

quizQuestion: Approximately how many meters long was Baryonyx?
explanation: Baryonyx was approximately 10 meters long and weighed around 1.7 tonnes. Source: Wikipedia/Baryonyx
statement: Baryonyx was approximately 10 meters long and weighed around 1.7 tonnes.
wowFactor: A 10-meter fish-eating dinosaur with a 31-cm thumb claw once waded through the swamps of what is now suburban Surrey, England.
visualDescription: Baryonyx scale comparison in a modern British setting showing its 10-meter length

quizQuestion: Which slender early dinosaur from the Late Triassic has its name meaning 'hollow form', from its hollow bones?
explanation: Coelophysis means 'hollow form', referring to its hollow bones. It is one of the earliest known dinosaurs, living approximately 213-203 million years ago. A spectacular mass death assemblage of hundreds of skeletons was found at Ghost Ranch, New Mexico. Source: Wikipedia/Coelophysis
statement: Coelophysis ('hollow form') is one of the earliest known dinosaurs. A mass death of hundreds of skeletons was found at Ghost Ranch, New Mexico.
wowFactor: Hundreds of Coelophysis skeletons were found piled together at Ghost Ranch, New Mexico — one of the most spectacular dinosaur bonebeds ever discovered.
visualDescription: Slender agile early theropod with hollow bones, dozens of them visible fossilized in a bonebed at Ghost Ranch, New Mexico

quizQuestion: In which geological period did Coelophysis live?
explanation: Coelophysis lived during the Late Triassic period, approximately 213-203 million years ago — making it one of the earliest known dinosaurs. Source: Wikipedia/Coelophysis
statement: Coelophysis lived during the Late Triassic period, 213-203 million years ago — making it one of the earliest known dinosaurs.
wowFactor: Coelophysis lived just after dinosaurs first evolved — it was among the pioneering species of the entire dinosaur lineage.
visualDescription: Coelophysis in a Late Triassic landscape with primitive conifers and ferns, before flowering plants existed

quizQuestion: Approximately how many meters long was Coelophysis?
explanation: Coelophysis was approximately 3 meters long and weighed about 25 kg. Despite being slender and small by later theropod standards, it was a fast and effective predator. Source: Wikipedia/Coelophysis
statement: Coelophysis was approximately 3 meters long and weighed about 25 kg — slender and fast.
wowFactor: Coelophysis was so slender that a 3-meter individual weighed only 25 kg — lighter than most adult humans.
visualDescription: Slender Coelophysis skeleton showing its hollow-boned, gracile build at 3 meters length

quizQuestion: Which fast-running omnivorous theropod from Mongolia has a name meaning 'chicken mimic' and was featured in a herd scene in Jurassic Park?
explanation: Gallimimus means 'chicken mimic', from its beak and overall body plan resembling a chicken scaled up enormously. It was an ornithomimid ('ostrich mimic' family) that could run at an estimated 40-70 km/h. It appeared in Jurassic Park's famous herd stampede scene. Source: Wikipedia/Gallimimus
statement: Gallimimus ('chicken mimic') was one of the fastest dinosaurs, estimated at 40-70 km/h, and appeared in Jurassic Park's famous herd scene.
wowFactor: Gallimimus had no teeth at all — just a toothless beak — despite being a large theropod in the same family as meat-eaters.
visualDescription: Ostrich-like dinosaur with long legs and a small toothless beak running at full sprint across a Mongolian plain

quizQuestion: In which geological period did Gallimimus live?
explanation: Gallimimus lived during the Late Cretaceous period, approximately 70 million years ago, in what is now the Nemegt Formation of Mongolia. Source: Wikipedia/Gallimimus
statement: Gallimimus lived during the Late Cretaceous period, approximately 70 million years ago, in Mongolia.
wowFactor: Gallimimus shared its Mongolian ecosystem with Tarbosaurus (an Asian relative of T. rex) — and its speed was likely its primary defense.
visualDescription: Gallimimus herd running across an open Late Cretaceous Mongolian steppe

quizQuestion: Approximately how many meters long was Gallimimus?
explanation: Gallimimus was approximately 6 meters long and weighed about 440 kg. Despite this size, it was toothless and likely omnivorous or herbivorous. Source: Wikipedia/Gallimimus
statement: Gallimimus was approximately 6 meters long and weighed about 440 kg — the largest ornithomimid known.
wowFactor: A 6-meter, 440-kg dinosaur with no teeth — Gallimimus was one of the largest toothless land animals to ever live.
visualDescription: Gallimimus shown at 6 meters length alongside a human figure and an ostrich for scale comparison

quizQuestion: Which bizarre herbivorous theropod from Mongolia had the longest claws of any known animal ever — up to 1 meter long?
explanation: Therizinosaurus had claws estimated up to 1 meter long — the longest claws of any known animal in Earth's history. Despite being a theropod in the same group as T. rex, it was entirely herbivorous with a pot belly. Its name means 'scythe lizard'. Source: Wikipedia/Therizinosaurus
statement: Therizinosaurus had the longest claws of any known animal ever — up to 1 meter long. Despite being a theropod, it was herbivorous.
wowFactor: Therizinosaurus had claws up to 1 meter long — longer than any other animal that has ever existed — yet it used them to pull down branches, not to hunt.
visualDescription: Enormous pot-bellied theropod with three massive scythe-like claws over a meter long, calmly pulling tree branches down to eat in a Mongolian forest

quizQuestion: In which geological period did Therizinosaurus live?
explanation: Therizinosaurus lived during the Late Cretaceous period, approximately 70-68 million years ago, in what is now Mongolia. Source: Wikipedia/Therizinosaurus
statement: Therizinosaurus lived during the Late Cretaceous period, 70-68 million years ago, in Mongolia.
wowFactor: Therizinosaurus shared its Mongolian ecosystem with both Velociraptor and Tarbosaurus — its enormous claws were likely defensive weapons against these predators.
visualDescription: Therizinosaurus in a Late Cretaceous Mongolian forest, its massive scythe claws clearly visible

quizQuestion: Approximately how many meters long was Therizinosaurus?
explanation: Therizinosaurus was approximately 10 meters long and weighed around 5 tonnes. Source: Wikipedia/Therizinosaurus
statement: Therizinosaurus was approximately 10 meters long and weighed around 5 tonnes.
wowFactor: A 10-meter, 5-tonne herbivore with 1-meter claws — Therizinosaurus was built like a giant sloth crossed with a dinosaur.
visualDescription: Therizinosaurus scale comparison showing its 10-meter length and massive pot-bellied build

quizQuestion: Despite being a theropod in the same group as T. rex, what was Therizinosaurus's diet?
explanation: Therizinosaurus was herbivorous — it ate plants despite being a theropod (the same clade as all large meat-eating dinosaurs). It used its enormous claws to pull down branches and its pot belly housed a large digestive system for plant matter. Source: Wikipedia/Therizinosaurus
statement: Therizinosaurus was herbivorous — a plant-eater — despite being a theropod in the same group as Tyrannosaurus.
wowFactor: Therizinosaurus is proof that the carnivorous theropod lineage could evolve giant herbivores — it gave up meat-eating entirely and became one of the largest plant-eaters of its time.
visualDescription: Therizinosaurus pot belly visible as it pulls leafy branches down, clearly showing its herbivorous nature despite its terrifying claws

quizQuestion: Which dinosaur's name means 'egg thief', though it was later found to be brooding its own eggs rather than stealing them?
explanation: Oviraptor means 'egg thief' — it was originally thought to be stealing Protoceratops eggs when first discovered by Roy Chapman Andrews in 1923. Later fossil evidence showed it was actually brooding its own eggs, completely vindicating the animal. Source: Wikipedia/Oviraptor
statement: Oviraptor means 'egg thief' but was actually vindicated — it was brooding its own eggs, not stealing them.
wowFactor: Oviraptor was wrongly accused of egg theft for 70 years. When scientists found its skeleton sitting on a nest of eggs, they realized it was a devoted parent — not a thief.
visualDescription: Oviraptor crouched protectively over a clutch of eggs in a brooding pose, its parrot-like beak and head crest visible

quizQuestion: In which geological period did Oviraptor live?
explanation: Oviraptor lived during the Late Cretaceous period, approximately 75-71 million years ago, in what is now Mongolia. Source: Wikipedia/Oviraptor
statement: Oviraptor lived during the Late Cretaceous period, 75-71 million years ago, in Mongolia.
wowFactor: Oviraptor lived at the same time and place as Velociraptor — both were found in the Djadochta Formation of Mongolia.
visualDescription: Oviraptor in a Late Cretaceous Mongolian desert landscape

quizQuestion: Approximately how many meters long was Oviraptor?
explanation: Oviraptor was approximately 1.5 meters long and weighed about 35 kg — roughly the size of a large dog. Source: Wikipedia/Oviraptor
statement: Oviraptor was approximately 1.5 meters long — roughly the size of a large dog.
wowFactor: Oviraptor was smaller than many people imagine — a 1.5-meter, 35-kg animal with a parrot beak and a head crest.
visualDescription: Oviraptor shown at 1.5 meters length beside a large dog for scale comparison

quizQuestion: Which American paleontologist-explorer led the expedition that first discovered Oviraptor in Mongolia in 1923?
explanation: Roy Chapman Andrews led the Central Asiatic Expeditions of the American Museum of Natural History in the 1920s. He discovered Oviraptor in Mongolia in 1923 along with many other significant fossil finds. Some say he inspired the Indiana Jones character. Source: Wikipedia/Oviraptor
statement: Roy Chapman Andrews discovered Oviraptor in Mongolia in 1923 during his Central Asiatic Expeditions.
wowFactor: Roy Chapman Andrews is sometimes cited as inspiration for Indiana Jones — he was a dashing adventurer-scientist who discovered dinosaurs across Asia in the 1920s.
visualDescription: Classic 1920s explorer-paleontologist excavating fossils in a Mongolian desert, wide-brimmed hat against the harsh sun
